agent_output: {"summary":{"verdict":"BLOCK","one_liner":"Critical security flaws: broken hash algorithm, timing-attack-vulnerable comparison, and unusable token lifecycle make this unfit for production B2B auth."},"critical_findings":[{"title":"MD5 is cryptographically broken — tokens are forgeable","risk":"MD5 has known collision and preimage attacks. An attacker who observes any token can forge valid tokens for arbitrary partners. NIST deprecated MD5 for security use in 2011.","severity":"Critical","location":"generatePartnerApiToken and validatePartnerApiToken — crypto.createHash('md5')","reproduction":"An attacker with one leaked token and knowledge of the partnerId can brute-force the timestamp (millisecond-precision, bounded to 30-day window = ~2.6B candidates) against the weak hash to recover the secretKey, then mint tokens for any partner.","fix":"Replace with HMAC-SHA256: `crypto.createHmac('sha256', secretKey).update(payload).digest('hex')`. This provides both a strong hash and proper keyed authentication. For long-lived tokens, consider JWT with RS256 or a proper opaque token stored server-side.","time_to_fix":"30 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Token security, partner trust, compliance (PCI/SOC2 likely require non-deprecated algorithms)"},{"title":"Timing-attack-vulnerable string comparison leaks token bytes","risk":"Using `===` for token comparison allows a timing side-channel attack. An attacker can iteratively guess each character of a valid token by measuring response times, fully recovering a valid token without knowing the secret.","severity":"Critical","location":"validatePartnerApiToken — `return token === expectedToken`","reproduction":"Attacker sends thousands of validation requests with tokens that match progressively more leading characters. Statistical analysis of response times reveals correct characters one byte at a time.","fix":"Use constant-time comparison: `return crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(token, 'hex'), Buffer.from(expectedToken, 'hex'))`. Also add a length check before calling timingSafeEqual since it throws on mismatched lengths.","time_to_fix":"10 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Token validation security"},{"title":"Generated token is unusable — timestamp is lost, validation always fails","risk":"generatePartnerApiToken uses Date.now() internally but never returns or persists the timestamp. validatePartnerApiToken requires the exact timestamp to reconstruct the expected token. Without knowing the precise millisecond, no caller can ever validate a token. This is a functional break, not just a design issue.","severity":"Critical","location":"generatePartnerApiToken return value; validatePartnerApiToken timestamp parameter","reproduction":"Call generatePartnerApiToken('partner1', 'secret'). Receive a hex string. Now attempt to call validatePartnerApiToken with that hex string — you cannot, because you don't know the millisecond-precision timestamp baked into the token.","fix":"Either (a) return a structured token that includes the timestamp (e.g., `base64(timestamp:hmac)`) so the validator can extract it, or (b) switch to a stateful model where generated tokens are stored in a database and validated by lookup. Option (a) is simpler: `const ts = Date.now(); return Buffer.from(JSON.stringify({ts, sig: hmac(partnerId + ':' + ts, secretKey)})).toString('base64');`","time_to_fix":"1 hour","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Core functionality — no token can be validated today"}],"important_issues":[{"title":"Secret key concatenation instead of HMAC keying","description":"Appending the secret to the payload (`payload + secretKey`) before hashing is a length-extension-vulnerable pattern. HMAC was designed to solve exactly this. Even after switching to SHA256, use HMAC, not hash(message+key).","location":"Both functions"},{"title":"No token expiry enforcement","description":"Tokens are described as 30-day-lived but there is zero expiry check in validatePartnerApiToken. Even if the timestamp were recoverable, nothing compares it against a max-age. An expired token validates forever.","location":"validatePartnerApiToken"},{"title":"No input validation on partnerId","description":"partnerId is concatenated directly into the payload with a colon delimiter. A partnerId containing ':' would create ambiguous payloads, potentially allowing one partner to forge tokens for another (e.g., partnerId 'A:1234' collides with partner 'A' at timestamp 1234...).","location":"generatePartnerApiToken payload construction"}],"suggestions":["Use a well-tested library (e.g., jsonwebtoken for JWTs, or uuid + database lookup for opaque tokens) rather than hand-rolling token generation for B2B integrations.","Add token revocation capability — for 30-day tokens, you need the ability to revoke a compromised token before expiry.","Log all token generation and validation events for audit trail — compliance frameworks require this for B2B API access.","Add rate limiting on the validation endpoint to mitigate brute-force and timing attacks."],"production_readiness":{"deployment_risks":"This code is fundamentally broken — tokens cannot be validated after generation. Deploying this would either break all partner integrations or force a workaround that bypasses validation entirely.","rollback_capability":"N/A — this should not be deployed.","backward_compatibility":"If any tokens were somehow issued with this code, migrating to a secure scheme will invalidate them. Plan a migration window with partners.","monitoring_coverage":"No logging, no metrics, no audit trail. B2B token operations must be auditable.","breaking_changes":{"api_breaking":true,"db_destructive":false,"shared_library":false,"auth_security":true,"data_pipeline":false,"config_changes":false,"details":["Token format will change when fixed — all existing integrations must re-authenticate","Switching from MD5 to HMAC-SHA256 changes token output format"]},"safe_to_deploy":false,"rollback_plan":"Do not deploy. Rewrite token generation using HMAC-SHA256 or JWT, include timestamp in token output, use constant-time comparison, add expiry checks, then review again."},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":97,"high_confidence_areas":["MD5 weakness is well-documented and indisputable","Timing attack via === is a known, exploitable vulnerability","Timestamp loss making validation impossible is provable by reading the code"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact brute-force feasibility depends on whether secretKey has high entropy — weak keys make MD5 exploitation trivial, strong keys buy some time but MD5 is still unacceptable"],"assumptions":["This code is the actual production implementation, not a simplified example","Tokens are transmitted over networks and stored by external partners","Standard security compliance requirements apply to B2B integrations"]}}
